comment_text: I remember my police scanner app went off and 30,000 people were tuned in to Joplin.  When I listened I heard them saying "leave five here" and "leave ten there."  It took me a minute to figure out that they were talking about body bags.  The voice in my head said "grab your gear and get to Joplin."   People always say "it's like a war zone."  It always bothered me because it never is.  Visually, Joplin was quite a bit like a war zone.  Ukraine is the worst thing i've ever witnessed.  Joplin is number two, but Joplin accomplished it in minutes.  That's saying something.  It was simultaneously horrific devastation and an amazing triumph of the human spirit.  The first thing I saw in the disaster zone was that somebody found a stick and an American flag, and hosted the flag.  Shortly therafter, I saw a destroyed house with a sign in front of that said "for sale fixer upper."  Triumph of the human spirit.  We spent the next week going through the rubble, searching for bodies etc. with a team of people from as far away as Japan who all just showed up to help.  A kid from Pittsburgh was on our team.  He skipped his high school graduation to be there and was reporting to basic training immediately thereafter.  Every disaster attracts amazing and giving people.  You wind up with friends you keep for the rest of your life.

video_title: The Twister: Caught in the Storm | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I remember this day. I lived in Springfield and worked a Mercy Hospital. We spent all night making room in the hospital because Joplin’s patients were being rushed. On top of the injured. The sky that day became otherworldly. I can only describe it as what looked like bubble wrap in greens, yellows and grays in the sky. It almost looked like you could touch them! We found X-rays from Mercy Joplin that had blown into the parking lot. Truly devastating.

comment_text:
I was working at the new (at the time) Orange Leaf FroYo on Rangeline back then. I was supposed to work 2-Close and my graduation (Webb City class of 2011) was supposed to be May 23 2011 (the following day). My mom came down from St. Louis for a surprise graduation party being hosted at my father's house in Oronogo so my boss let me leave work early. I still remember the sunset. The clouds looked like a thousand funnel cakes that had been unravelled and stretched across the sky, it was absolutely beautiful. 

No major damage to my workplace, but the pet shop (that was no longer in business) in the same plaza 2 doors down had the whole front of the building ripped off. I was lucky to have been allowed to leave work when I did. Anything could have happened.

I wanted to also say that FEMA really did a lot of people dirty. My aunt, uncle, and their 5 children lost their house, the two trailers on their property, and their vehicles and were given $7,000.

Thanks for letting me share. I'll never forget.

comment_text: The part about looking up and seeing blue sky is inaccurate though. Tornadoes are not like hurricanes. The funnel is about 10% that we see coming down from the cloud base of the supercell. The supercell is anvil shaped because as the storm and updraft strengthen it pushes up against a layer of cold air in the atmosphere called the inversion. Eventually the updraft bursts through that inversion and explodes upwards and strong upper level winds spread it out like the top of an anvil.  There is no hole that opens at the top of the storm.  So depictions of being able to look up into the funnel and see sky (like in the movie Twister and Into the Storm) are not realistic.

comment_text: I was on my way to work and it was a beautiful afternoon. I shut the radio off and put in a CD because every station was talking about an incoming storm and a possibility of a tornado. I was at work for maybe 15 minutes when we had to head for the basement. We were joking and laughing. Even when the power went out, we were still making jokes. Then there was an incredibly loud sound that went on for minutes, like something was trying to claw its way inside. Then it was gone and it was silent. Outside it was raining so hard that water started coming in under the door and then along the door frame. It was like we were being submerged, I've never seen anything like it. We were lucky because we were on the very edge and the building had damage to the upper floors, the windows were blown out, but otherwise okay. When we went outside, we could see smoke rising from a few places, but couldn't tell where it was coming from. One of my coworkers had an police scanner app on his phone and we could hear the police and fire department on it - they were overwhelmed and there were times we could hear people crying and screaming in the background. The casual attitude about tornadoes changed for us that day.

comment_text: This event was genuinely frightening. I remember I was riding my bike with my friends. I was always told to not go far from the house, but I did anyway. We were in my friends yard when the sky got dark. We thought it was just going to be rain so we went inside my friends house to chill out. Then my friends mom told us a tornado touched down somewhere so I was riding home when I saw the tornado. It was huge, something I would only see in a dream or a nightmare. Then, when I was almost home, a tree limb that was pre-damaged fell on me and I remember I was there for hours until people saw me and helped free my body. It's still traumatizing every time I here the sirens go off. Rest In Peace to the victims who lost there lives, I got lucky.

video_title: The Twister: Caught in the Storm | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Have you Never heard of this? 
Its ok. 2011 was an extremely intense year. Especially April and May.

2011, the super tornado outbreak  and the year the  tsunami hit Japan and had the nuclear plant incident. Just a month prior. So there was a lot going on in the news. Between the other tornados in April and the tsunami, then this freak of nature. They still don't tell the whole truth about the Joplin Tornado. The anemometer broke at St John's Hospital at over 330 mph. It moved the entire Hospital off its foundation! A man was sucked out the hospital window and found almost a mile away. There were more than 500 body parts found. Refrigerated tractor trailers were brought in as temp storage for parts and bodies. There were many FEMA agents that lived in the path of this storm the year before. I think many more were killed than what they reported. Something is off with this one. 
Debri from Joplin reached the city limits of Rolla, Missouri. (180 miles East of Joplin) Debri rained on Springfield, Missouri from Joplin. 
There were talks of changing the fujita scale for this one. Rumors of calling it an F6 or F7. 
The sights, the screams, the smells, will never be forgotten. Death lingered in the air for months. 
The Joplin high school graduation took place at the college across town that day. 
EMS were warned about potential threat of a large outbreak of tornados for that day. But i dont know if the public knew of then potential outbreak. It was a beautiful sunny day. Blue skies. A little muggy, i had been laying sod all day. And just finished a little after 5pm. We were going to run up to Pizza Inn on Main st, for our favorite pizza, but we were pretty filthy from our task. So we headed back to the house to change. When i parked the truck, the tornado sirens started going off...

Im done talking about it.

The ABC show Extreme makeover, home edition,  built those 7 houses in 7 days, a block from where I lived in Joplin during the tornado. 
I lost a cousin to this tornado. 
RIP to all those lost.

comment_text:
I hope they mention the butterfly people. My whole family were affected by the storm. I was in Texas at the time but drove down the very next day, I will never forget how scary it was. It looked like a war zone. It was odd not to know where anything was. Somehow the path of the tornado stopped short of my childhood home about four blocks away and took a ninety degree turn away. My cousin and his wife’s home were not so lucky,  their home was completely destroyed. But the countless stories of children seeing butterfly people are incredible and I hope they included this.
We currently live right where the path went through in a home that was recently constructed. I miss the trees that lined this street the most. They were old and glorious. 
Rip to all the peoples whose lives were taken to soon. And whose lives were permanently shaken. Those who still bare the marks physically, emotionally and financially. The life in the city still has a certain cloud. Although much has changed for the better, this grave event still haunts and lingers.

video_title: The Twister: Caught in the Storm | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I WAS THERE! I remember it thoroughly because that’s how I met my wife. The sky turned green, the sirens screamed, and Joplin disappeared into chaos. I should’ve taken cover, but I panicked and ran straight into the storm.

And so did Emily.

She was my secret crush, the girl I’d never had the guts to talk to. But there we were, sprinting side by side as the wind tore the world apart. A pickup truck flew past. A house folded like paper.

Grandpa once said, “If you ever get caught, run for the eye.” So I grabbed Emily’s hand and did just that.

The wind lifted us, pulling us into the storm’s grip. For a second, I thought we were done. But then, silence.

We were in the eye. Floating debris spun around us, but inside, it was calm. Emily looked at me, eyes wide. “What now?” she whispered.

Then the eye shifted.

I woke up blocks away, tangled in a tree. Emily landed in a pile of mattresses. Somehow, we both survived.

Years later, at our wedding, she whispered, “I married the guy who ran into a tornado with me.”

Grandpa was right. The storm took everything but it gave me Emily.
I’m so thankful for that storm, and if you saw Emily’s booty you would know why.

comment_text:
I remember watching this tornado form...I was in Treece Kansas. We were standing outside my friend Carolynn's trailer. The clouds were spinning and they looked like little pins in a pincushion... But they would go up into the cloud and come back down bigger and bigger. I looked at Carolynn and said "if that tornado touches down, it will be absolutely devastating"...I hate that I was right. We had just lived through Picher tornado a couple of years prior, so the trauma from that tornado was still fresh. I lived on college Street in Picher. Storms still make me nervous. I bought a brick house with a storm cellar and it helps. Most on our block know that they can use our cellar, whether or not we're home. We're one of maybe 3 houses with a cellar on our street. 

I used to sit outside and watch the storms. I'm getting to where I'm about to do that again now, but being a parent hits different. I was a teen when Picher was hit and a young adult for Joplin. I couldn't imagine going through that with children.
